iran_crude: Iran's crude exports collapsed 38% from 12,920 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 8,000 kbd (23–28 Mar), with China — its sole visible buyer at 4,604 kbd in early March — completely disappearing from destination data after the USAF struck Kharg Island military targets on Mar 13. While Kharg's oil infrastructure was spared, the explicit US warning that crude facilities could be next has likely deterred Chinese tankers from loading, even as Iran handles 94% of its ~1.68 mb/d exports through the terminal.

The loss of Chinese buying visibility is compounded by Hormuz closure logistics: Iran's ship-to-ship transfer network — which handles ~68% of its shadow-fleet crude movements — faces acute operational risk with over 150 tankers stranded outside the strait. Wanhua Chemical's FM declaration on Middle East supplies and Cosco Shipping's cautious resumption of Gulf bookings (Mar 25) signal that Chinese buyers are diversifying away from Iranian barrels at the margin, even as Russia captures the redirected demand.

With total flows still at 8,000 kbd in the latest week, Iran retains significant export capacity despite the crisis — likely routing via non-Hormuz-dependent paths or pre-positioned floating storage. However, if the US escalates to strikes on Kharg itself, as warned, Iranian exports could collapse to near-zero, removing a further ~1.5 mb/d from an already deficit global market where Brent carries an $18/bbl geopolitical risk premium.
iran_lpg: Iran's LPG exports fell 35% from 63 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 41 ktons (23–28 Mar), with both China (41 ktons) and India (22 ktons) disappearing entirely as destinations after the first week. This is consistent with the broader Hormuz closure impact: India's Gujarat Gas has imposed 50% supply cuts to industrial customers under its own FM declaration, while China's PDH operators are scrambling for US and Australian replacement cargoes.

The remaining ~23–41 ktons of weekly volume has no identified destination, suggesting Iranian LPG is moving through sanctions-evasion channels (ship-to-ship transfers, relabelling) that obscure final buyers. With ~30% of Iran's southern NGL-linked oil fields shut in and Hormuz transit effectively blocked, recovery potential is limited until either the strait reopens or alternative overland/pipeline routes are established — neither of which appears imminent.
iraq_crude: Iraq's crude exports suffered a catastrophic 90% collapse from 15,283 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 1,500 kbd (23–28 Mar), reflecting the full force of the Oil Ministry's Mar 20 FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields. Basra output has crashed to ~800 kb/d from 3.3 mb/d as storage fills and southern terminals are shut — with BP ordered to cut North Rumaila to 350 kb/d. All four major seaborne buyers — China (4,841 kbd), India (1,179 kbd), Italy (998 kbd), and Malaysia (998 kbd) — have been completely severed.

The sole lifeline is the reactivated Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline to Turkey, which appeared as a new route in the 16–22 Mar data at 1,500 kbd and held steady through 23–28 Mar. According to The National, Iraq resumed exports at an initial 100–140 kb/d on Mar 18, stabilising at ~170–250 kb/d — though the Kpler data suggests actual loaded volumes may be somewhat higher. This is a fraction of pre-crisis exports but represents Iraq's only functioning outlet.

The pipeline contract with Turkey expires in July 2026, creating additional political risk. Kurdistan Region fields could add 200 kb/d to Ceyhan flows, but Baghdad-KRG financial disputes remain unresolved. With 7.9 mb/d of Gulf crude shut in across all producers, Iraq's near-total reliance on a single pipeline route through an unstable region makes its export recovery among the most fragile of any Gulf state.
iraq_naphtha: Iraq naphtha exports are sporadic and minimal — 0 to 104 kbd across the four-week period with no consistent destination data. This reflects the near-total collapse of Iraqi refining and NGL processing following the FM declaration, as the feedstock base from Basra crude has been cut by 76%. With YNCC (South Korea) and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) both declaring FM on petrochemical production due to Middle East naphtha supply loss, even a recovery in Iraqi output would face a market where downstream buyers have already pivoted to alternative sources.
kuwait_crude: Kuwait's crude exports have effectively ceased — falling from 8,135 kbd (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week (23–28 Mar), a 100% decline. KPC's FM declaration on crude exports from a 2.6 mb/d baseline is now fully reflected in the flow data, with China (the sole identified buyer at 2,000 kbd in early March) completely cut off.

The destruction is physical, not just logistical: Iranian drones struck the Al-Ahmadi refinery (730 kb/d) on Mar 19–20, igniting fires across multiple processing units, and Kafco fuel storage was destroyed. Unlike Saudi Arabia, Kuwait has no bypass pipeline to the Red Sea, leaving it entirely Hormuz-dependent. The brief 546 kbd uptick in 16–22 Mar appears to have been residual storage drawdown rather than a sustainable flow.

Kuwait is one of the most exposed Gulf producers — with zero pipeline alternatives, destroyed refining capacity, and complete dependence on Hormuz reopening for any export recovery. The IEA estimates the world has lost 4.5–5 mb/d of oil from the war, and Kuwait's total shutdown is a significant contributor to the crisis that could see losses double by mid-April if the strait remains closed.
kuwait_sulphur: Kuwait's sulphur exports are negligible — peaking at just 18 ktons in two of the four weeks and falling to zero in the latest period (23–28 Mar). Sulphur production is a byproduct of oil and gas processing at Al-Ahmadi, which was struck by drones and is offline indefinitely. No recovery is possible until refining operations resume.
oman_crude: Oman stands out as the Gulf's most resilient crude exporter, with flows essentially flat at 7,632 kbd (23–28 Mar) versus 7,800 kbd (2–8 Mar) — just a 2.2% decline. This resilience is structural: Oman's Mina Al-Fahal terminal sits outside the Strait of Hormuz, and its inland oil production (predominantly from Petroleum Development Oman's fields) was largely unaffected despite Iranian drone strikes on Salalah port oil tanks on Mar 11.

China remains the overwhelming buyer, taking 7,632 kbd of the 7,632 kbd total in the latest week — effectively 100% of Oman's crude exports. Taiwan emerged briefly (1,885 kbd in 16–22 Mar) but dropped out in the latest week. Oman's non-Hormuz position gives it a strategic premium: with 4.5–5 mb/d of Gulf crude offline, Oman's barrels are among the few Middle Eastern cargoes that can physically reach Asian buyers without transit risk.

Watch for Oman to punch above its weight in the coming weeks — Argus Media reports that Oman's non-Hormuz position gives it a strategic export advantage, and Chinese refiners desperate for sour crude alternatives to sanctioned Iranian and disrupted Saudi/Iraqi barrels may bid Oman grades to significant premiums over pre-crisis levels.
oman_lng: Oman LNG exports fell 51% from 303 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 148 ktons (23–28 Mar), but crucially this facility remains one of the only Middle Eastern LNG operations still running after Qatar's total 77 Mtpa shutdown removed ~20% of global LNG supply. The decline likely reflects operational limitations rather than conflict damage — Oman's 10.4 Mtpa facility at Qalhat is outside Hormuz.

Buyer rotation has been dramatic: South Korea (77 ktons) and Taiwan (71 ktons) disappeared from the latest week, while Thailand appeared as a new buyer (35 ktons in 23–28 Mar). India remains the anchor customer at 77 ktons, down from 154 ktons in early March — consistent with Petronet LNG's FM declaration as India scrambles for alternative supplies. With Asian spot LNG prices up ~39% and European TTF up 52%, Oman's remaining cargoes are commanding massive premiums.

The narrowing buyer base — from 3 destinations in early March to just 2 (India and Thailand) — suggests Oman is prioritising long-term contract buyers over spot, or that its limited volumes are being competed for intensely. South Korea's exit is notable given KOGAS's recent 10-year deal with Trafigura for US LNG, suggesting Korean buyers are actively pivoting away from Middle Eastern dependence.
oman_kero_jet: Oman's jet/kerosene exports are sporadic and low-volume — 58 kbd to Mauritius in 2–8 Mar, a 172 kbd cargo to Djibouti in 16–22 Mar, and zero in the latest week. These appear to be opportunistic spot cargoes from Oman's Sohar and Duqm refineries rather than a structural trade flow. The Djibouti cargo likely reflects military/bunkering demand given the US naval presence in the region.
oman_gasoil_diesel: Oman's gasoil/diesel exports declined 32% from 1,005 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 682 kbd (23–28 Mar), with a dramatic reshuffling of destinations. The initial broad customer base — Namibia (345 kbd), Argentina (320 kbd), Madagascar (167 kbd), South Africa (115 kbd), and Mauritius (58 kbd) — has been replaced by Pakistan (351 kbd) and Mauritius (331 kbd) in the latest week.

Pakistan's emergence as the top diesel buyer reflects the acute fuel crisis hitting Hormuz-dependent importers: with Saudi, Kuwaiti, and UAE refined product exports severely disrupted, Pakistan is turning to non-Hormuz Omani supply. The disappearance of Latin American and African buyers (Argentina, Namibia, Madagascar) suggests these cargoes have been redirected to higher-premium regional markets where supply urgency is greatest. Oman's Duqm refinery (230 kbd capacity), located outside Hormuz on the Arabian Sea coast, is the likely source of these flows.
oman_gasoline: Oman's gasoline exports rose 16% to 843 kbd (23–28 Mar), making it one of the few Gulf product flows showing growth. The most striking development is Saudi Arabia appearing as a buyer for the first time, taking 332 kbd (39% of total) — a clear signal that even the kingdom's massive refining complex cannot meet domestic demand as it redirects crude volumes to Yanbu for export.

Pakistan returned as the top buyer at 511 kbd, consistent with the broader pattern of Hormuz-dependent countries scrambling for Omani products. With MRPL (India) cutting 500 kb/d of refinery runs under FM and regional gasoline production severely constrained by refinery shutdowns (Bapco Sitra destroyed, Al-Ahmadi struck), Oman's non-Hormuz position makes its Sohar and Duqm refineries critical swing suppliers for the entire Indian Ocean region.
qatar_crude: Qatar's crude exports have effectively ceased — dropping from 2,047 kbd (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week (23–28 Mar), consistent with QatarEnergy's comprehensive FM on all hydrocarbon exports. All offshore oil fields including the major Al Shaheen block (~440 kb/d) have been shut since early March along with Laffan Refineries 1 and 2.

The only blip was 822 kbd in 16–22 Mar, with the UAE as the sole buyer (449 kbd, 55% of total) — likely a residual cargo from floating storage or a short-pipeline transfer between the two neighbouring states. This has not repeated, confirming the shutdown is total. Qatar's crude production was always secondary to its LNG operations, but the loss of ~440 kb/d adds to the 7.9 mb/d of Gulf crude shut-in estimated by the IEA.
qatar_lng: Qatar LNG exports have collapsed 65% from 370 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 129 ktons (23–28 Mar), with the world's largest LNG producer — 77 Mtpa, representing ~20% of global supply — effectively offline since Iranian drone attacks hit Ras Laffan and Mesaieed on Mar 2. Asian spot prices surged past $25/MMBtu (+39%) and European TTF spiked 52% within 24 hours of the shutdown, marking the largest global gas supply shock since the 2022 Russia-Ukraine crisis.

China (95 ktons in early March) has been completely cut off as QatarEnergy extended FM to Chinese contracts on Mar 24. The only remaining flows are small volumes to Kuwait (68 ktons in 23–28 Mar) — likely pipeline or short-distance transfers for Kuwait's desperate domestic power generation needs, given that Kuwait's own Al-Zour LNG import FSRU faces acute supply uncertainty.

The FM has cascaded through global supply chains: Shell declared FM on Qatar-sourced LNG to Asia/Europe clients, TotalEnergies invoked FM on 5.2 Mtpa of offtake, and Petronet LNG (India) declared FM to local buyers. Edison (Italy) and KOGAS (South Korea) have also been notified. The 129 ktons still flowing may represent pre-loaded cargoes or emergency allocations rather than new production — a full restart is impossible until the conflict ends and facility damage is assessed.
qatar_sulphur: Qatar's sulphur exports have collapsed from 72 ktons (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week, mirroring the total shutdown of LNG and gas processing operations at Ras Laffan. Sulphur is a byproduct of gas sweetening in LNG production — with all 77 Mtpa of Qatar's LNG offline, sulphur output is mechanically zero. Global sulphur markets, already tight, face additional pressure as Qatar's ~5 Mt/year of production (one of the world's largest) remains offline indefinitely.
saudi_arabia_crude: Saudi crude exports declined 39% from 44,659 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 27,115 kbd (23–28 Mar), but the kingdom has staged a remarkable partial recovery from the 19,880 kbd trough in 9–15 Mar by activating its East-West Pipeline at full 7 mb/d capacity. Fortune reports the pipeline reached its 7 million barrel/day goal by Mar 28, with ~5 mb/d now being exported through Yanbu on the Red Sea — up from just 1.1 mb/d in February, a 350% surge.

The customer mix has been fundamentally reshuffled by the crisis. Egypt — the dominant buyer at 16,191 kbd in early March (likely Suez transit-related) — has disappeared entirely, along with South Korea (4,000 kbd) and Japan (2,000 kbd). China has surged to become the top buyer at 7,706 kbd (23–28 Mar), up from 2,555 kbd in the first week. India has also grown strongly to 7,201 kbd. Myanmar appeared as a new destination at 2,010 kbd, likely serving as a re-export hub for Chinese-linked refineries.

The disappearance of Japan and South Korea — both IEA members who collectively released 102.5 mb from strategic reserves — suggests these buyers are drawing on emergency stocks rather than competing for limited Yanbu-routed Saudi barrels. The Red Sea export route carries its own risks: BloombergNEF warns that Iran has threatened Saudi's Red Sea infrastructure, and the Ras Tanura shutdown (Mar 2–13) demonstrated the vulnerability of loading facilities.

Saudi Arabia's export trajectory has stabilised at ~27,000 kbd, roughly 60% of pre-crisis levels — consistent with the pipeline's physical capacity constraints. Further upside is limited unless Hormuz reopens, as the East-West Pipeline is already at maximum throughput.
saudi_arabia_lpg: Saudi LPG exports remain severely depressed — rising from zero (2–8 Mar) to just 23 ktons (23–28 Mar), with India as the sole destination. Pre-crisis Saudi LPG exports typically ran at several hundred ktons/week, making this a >90% reduction. The curtailment reflects two factors: reduced NGL throughput from offshore field closures and shut-ins, and the prioritisation of pipeline capacity for crude oil over lighter products.

India's emergence as the sole buyer is notable: with Qatar's LPG (entirely a byproduct of LNG production) fully halted and UAE's Ruwais Refinery-2 offline after drone strikes, India's Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala cooking fuel programme faces acute supply risk. Even at 23 ktons/week, this is a fraction of India's needs — the government may need to source replacement volumes from the US Gulf Coast at significantly higher prices.
saudi_arabia_kero_jet: Saudi jet fuel/kerosene exports collapsed from 698 kbd (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week (23–28 Mar). The UK was the dominant buyer at 667 kbd in the first week, with Netherlands (320 kbd) taking over in 16–22 Mar — both likely reflecting Yanbu-to-Europe Red Sea/Suez routing. The complete cessation suggests Saudi refineries are either diverting jet fuel to domestic use (military operations) or lacking the crude feedstock to maintain product output as the East-West Pipeline prioritises raw crude for export.
With global jet fuel markets already tight and 3+ mb/d of Gulf refining capacity offline, the loss of Saudi jet exports will put upward pressure on crack spreads globally.
saudi_arabia_gasoil_diesel: Saudi diesel/gasoil exports declined 32% from 5,201 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 3,559 kbd (23–28 Mar), with a dramatic geographic pivot. Traditional European buyers — France (988 kbd), Albania (822 kbd), Spain (741 kbd), and Italy (680 kbd) — have all disappeared, replaced by Belgium (744 kbd), Pakistan (306 kbd), and Sudan (133 kbd). Egypt remains the largest single buyer but has halved from 657 kbd to 762 kbd.

The disappearance of France is notable given CMA CGM's FM on Hormuz and Suez transits and Maersk's suspension of all Hormuz routes — suggesting that shipping logistics, not just crude supply, are constraining European deliveries. Belgium's emergence as the top new buyer likely reflects ARA (Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp) hub restocking as Europe scrambles for diesel ahead of potential further supply losses. Pakistan and Sudan represent emergency allocations to Hormuz-dependent nations facing acute fuel crises.

The shift from European to regional/African buyers signals that Saudi refining output, routed via Yanbu, is being allocated to the most supply-constrained markets first — a de facto triage of global diesel demand.
saudi_arabia_gasoline: Saudi gasoline exports fell 61% from 1,565 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 613 kbd (23–28 Mar), with virtually the entire pre-crisis buyer base — Malaysia (325 kbd), South Africa (300 kbd), Singapore (345 kbd), Pakistan (248 kbd), Jordan (46 kbd) — disappearing. Only Singapore (300 kbd) and Kenya (313 kbd) remain in the latest week.

The mid-month spike to 1,924 kbd (16–22 Mar) was driven by Singapore (1,011 kbd) — likely distressed cargo procurement for the region's largest trading hub. Kenya's emergence as a buyer (313 kbd) is unusual and may reflect re-export or strategic stockpiling. The 61% decline in gasoline exports reflects the broader crude-over-products prioritisation at Yanbu: with pipeline capacity maxed at 7 mb/d, Saudi Arabia is choosing to export crude rather than refine and export products.

MRPL (India) has cut 500 kb/d of refinery runs under FM, and Bapco's Sitra refinery (405 kb/d) is destroyed — removing significant Asian and Gulf refining capacity and creating gasoline shortages that Saudi's reduced exports cannot fill.
saudi_arabia_naphtha: Saudi naphtha exports surged 443% from 94 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 510 kbd (23–28 Mar), making it one of the few Gulf export categories showing strong growth. Indonesia has become the dominant buyer at 510 kbd in the latest week — consistent with Chandra Asri's FM on petrochemical production due to Middle East naphtha loss, suggesting a direct bilateral supply arrangement to support Indonesia's cracker operations.

Japan (94 kbd in early March) has disappeared, while Indonesia and Singapore rotated through as buyers. The surge likely reflects Yanbu-routed naphtha from Saudi NGL processing at Rabigh or Jubail — products that would normally move east through Hormuz are now rerouted west and then around to Southeast Asia, at significantly higher freight costs. With YNCC (South Korea) also under FM for naphtha supply, Saudi naphtha barrels command premium pricing in a severely supply-constrained Asian petchem market.
saudi_arabia_sulphur: Saudi sulphur exports declined from 74 ktons (2–8 Mar) to zero in the latest week, with both Indonesia (52 ktons) and Lithuania (23 ktons) dropping out as buyers. Sulphur is a byproduct of crude processing and refining — with Saudi refineries reducing throughput to prioritise crude exports via the East-West Pipeline, sulphur production has fallen correspondingly. This adds to the global sulphur shortage alongside Qatar's ~5 Mt/year sulphur production being offline.
uae_crude: UAE crude exports declined 14% from 22,343 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 19,227 kbd (23–28 Mar), but staged a strong recovery from the 9,265 kbd trough in 16–22 Mar — suggesting ADNOC's ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah (1 mb/d capacity) and Habshan gas complex restart (Mar 23) are partially restoring export capability. The overall decline is less severe than peers, reflecting the UAE's partial pipeline bypass capacity.

The buyer reshuffling has been dramatic: Thailand (3,850–5,800 kbd in early weeks), Singapore (2,608 kbd), China (1,800 kbd), and Malaysia (1,054 kbd) have all disappeared from the latest week. India has emerged as the top buyer at 5,595 kbd — up from zero in early March — as ADNOC redirects Murban crude to India's refiners who are losing Iraqi and Kuwaiti supply. South Korea returned at 3,850 kbd, Japan appeared at 1,900 kbd, and the Philippines at 1,000 kbd — all likely purchasing via the Fujairah terminal.

The Fujairah hub itself faces ongoing risk: drone attacks on the port disrupted loading operations, and both MEE and Pearl Marine declared FM on bunker fuel supply contracts. Habshan resumed Mar 23 after a 4-day emergency shutdown, but Shah Gas Field remains shut and Ruwais Refinery-2 is offline after strikes. The 19,227 kbd recovery is encouraging but fragile — a second strike on Fujairah or ADCOP pipeline infrastructure could collapse UAE exports to near-zero.

Watch India's growing share of UAE crude: at 5,595 kbd (29% of total), India is now ADNOC's largest customer, up from zero before the crisis. This structural shift — driven by the loss of Iraqi and Kuwaiti supply — may persist post-conflict as India seeks to diversify away from Russian crude dependence.
uae_naphtha: UAE naphtha exports fell 42% from 557 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 323 kbd (23–28 Mar), with China (557 kbd in early March) completely disappearing and Taiwan emerging as the sole buyer at 323 kbd. The two middle weeks (9–22 Mar) saw zero naphtha exports, coinciding with the Habshan gas complex emergency shutdown and Ruwais Refinery-2 going offline after drone strikes.

Taiwan's emergence as the buyer likely reflects Formosa Petrochemical's urgent need for feedstock as Middle Eastern naphtha supply collapses across the board. The 323 kbd recovery in the latest week is consistent with the Habshan restart on Mar 23, but with Shah Gas Field still shut, NGL-linked naphtha production remains well below capacity. The Asian petchem sector faces sustained feedstock tightness until Gulf processing capacity is restored.
eu_27_crude: EU-27 crude exports collapsed 67% from 3,047 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 1,006 kbd (23–28 Mar), reflecting the cascading impact of the Hormuz crisis on European refinery economics rather than direct supply disruption. The 2,132 kbd Brazil-bound cargo in early March — likely a one-off North Sea or Mediterranean term lifting — did not repeat, and Turkey (324 kbd in 16–22 Mar) also dropped out as Ankara pivoted to cheaper Russian Urals barrels.

The Netherlands emerged as a new destination (407 kbd in 23–28 Mar), likely reflecting intra-EU refinery-to-storage transfers at Rotterdam as European traders reposition crude inventories ahead of tightening global supply. Finland's steady ~599 kbd across all four weeks suggests a locked-in term contract, possibly Kazakh CPC Blend via Baltic terminals.

With Brent hovering near $92/bbl — up $20/bbl for the month per IEA's March Oil Market Report — EU refiners face compressed margins that discourage discretionary crude re-exports. Watch for further contraction if the partial Hormuz reopening (Iran granted transit to China, Russia, India, Iraq, and Pakistan vessels on 26 Mar) diverts Asian buying power away from Atlantic Basin grades.
eu_27_lpg: EU-27 LPG exports fell 62.5% from 32 ktons (2–8 Mar) to just 12 ktons (23–28 Mar), a small-volume flow that nonetheless signals tightening regional balances. Poland remains the dominant buyer across all weeks (6.9–12.4 ktons), consistent with its role as a major EU LPG import hub for heating and petrochemical use.

Lebanon (4.6 ktons in early March) and Turkey (3.6 ktons) disappeared from the destination list as the Hormuz crisis disrupted Eastern Mediterranean shipping logistics — Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have both declared FM on Gulf shipping loops with Cape rerouting, making short-haul Med trades less economical. France appeared as a new minor destination (0.8 ktons), possibly reflecting CMA CGM's suspended Hormuz/Suez transits redirecting domestic supply chains within Europe.
eu_27_kero_jet: EU-27 jet fuel exports cratered 85.7% from 483 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 69 kbd (23–28 Mar), the steepest decline in this batch. This aligns with OilPrice.com reports that Europe faces acute jet fuel price surges and supply shortages as Middle Eastern kerosene supply — traditionally flowing from Gulf refineries to European aviation hubs — has been severed by the Hormuz closure.

Denmark's disappearance as a buyer (133 kbd in early March, zero since) coincides with Maersk's FM declaration and suspended Hormuz transit, which has disrupted Danish refinery product logistics. The 16–22 Mar week saw diversification to South Africa (169 kbd) and Cyprus (118 kbd), suggesting EU refiners are seeking non-traditional outlets, but these flows did not sustain into the latest week.

Only UK-bound volumes (69 kbd) remain in 23–28 Mar, likely intra-European pipeline or short-sea transfers. With Middle Eastern jet fuel offline and EU refiners cutting runs due to elevated crude costs, the aviation sector faces sustained tightness heading into the spring travel season.
eu_27_gasoil_diesel: EU-27 diesel exports peaked at 7,696 kbd (9–15 Mar) before declining 66% to 2,582 kbd (23–28 Mar), a dramatic contraction driven by soaring domestic retention as European diesel prices exceed EUR 2/litre in Germany, Finland, France, Italy, and the Netherlands. With the Hormuz closure removing ~20 mb/d of Gulf flows including significant Middle Eastern diesel supply, EU refiners are prioritising domestic markets over exports.

The destination mix has shifted structurally: UK, historically the top buyer at 966–1,431 kbd, disappeared from the top 5 in the latest week as Croatia (544 kbd) and Slovenia (356 kbd) emerged — likely reflecting Central/Southern European inland demand pulling diesel from Mediterranean refineries. Poland dropped from a peak of 1,714 kbd (9–15 Mar) to 345 kbd, consistent with filling strategic reserves early in the crisis.

Germany's disappearance from the destination list is notable given Hapag-Lloyd's FM declaration on Gulf shipping — German importers may be switching to pipeline-supplied Russian diesel despite sanctions pressure, or drawing down domestic stocks. The IEA's assessment that EU diesel shortages are manageable in the medium term assumes continued Russian seaborne diesel flows, which remain vulnerable to further sanctions enforcement.
eu_27_gasoline: EU-27 gasoline exports have been the most resilient product flow in the bloc, rising 4.8% over four weeks to 6,867 kbd (23–28 Mar) from 6,555 kbd (2–8 Mar), as European refineries — running lighter slates due to elevated crude costs — produce gasoline-heavy yields that must find export markets.

The geographic pivot is striking: Libya (912 kbd in early March, the top buyer) dropped to 282 kbd as the country's own refinery capacity and import logistics shifted. West Africa has surged as the primary growth outlet — Ghana (345 kbd), Senegal (345 kbd), and Mauritania (319 kbd) all appeared as new top-5 destinations in 23–28 Mar, filling the vacuum left by halted Gulf-origin gasoline flows to the region.

Spain's rise from 190 kbd to 413 kbd as the top destination in the latest week reflects surging Iberian pump prices (up 27% per IRU data, reaching EUR 1.79/litre), which are making Spanish domestic demand a pull factor for EU-produced gasoline. The Saudi Arabia cargo (522 kbd in 9–15 Mar) — a rare EU-to-Gulf flow — was likely a crisis arbitrage play that has not repeated as Hormuz transit risks remain prohibitive.
eu_27_naphtha: EU-27 naphtha exports declined 31.7% from 2,064 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,409 kbd (23–28 Mar), driven by the collapse of Belgium and Netherlands as destinations — traditionally intra-EU transfers to ARA petrochemical crackers that have now curtailed operations due to elevated feedstock costs and downstream demand destruction.

Asian buyers have become prominent: China took 345 kbd and Japan 352 kbd in the middle weeks as YNCC (South Korea) and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) declared FM on petrochemical production due to total Middle Eastern naphtha supply suspension. EU naphtha is filling a critical gap — but at significantly higher delivered costs given the Atlantic Basin-to-Asia freight premium.

Italy's emergence as the top destination (502 kbd in 23–28 Mar) suggests Italian petrochemical complexes at Porto Marghera and Priolo are maintaining operations by sourcing from Northern European refineries rather than their traditional Gulf suppliers. Watch for further Asian pull on EU naphtha if the Hormuz partial reopening (limited to Chinese, Russian, Indian, Iraqi, and Pakistani vessels) fails to restore Middle Eastern naphtha supply.
russia_crude: Russian crude exports surged 41% from 33,451 kbd (2–8 Mar) to a peak of 47,314 kbd (9–15 Mar) as Moscow capitalised on the Hormuz crisis to capture market share from stranded Gulf barrels. China absorbed the largest volumes at 18,027 kbd in that peak week — nearly 50% above pre-crisis levels — consistent with CREA data showing China now accounts for 52% of Russia's fossil fuel export revenue.

The subsequent decline to 28,658 kbd (23–28 Mar) reflects both logistics constraints (Reuters reports at least 40% of Russia's export capacity halted due to Ukrainian drone attacks on infrastructure and tanker seizures) and potentially the 26 Mar Hormuz partial reopening, which granted transit to Chinese, Indian, and Russian-flagged vessels — creating the possibility that some Asian buyers may begin rotating back to Gulf grades.

Turkey's dramatic rise as a destination — from zero in early March to 3,630 kbd (16–22 Mar) and 2,163 kbd (23–28 Mar) — marks a strategic pivot by Ankara to secure discounted Russian Urals as traditional Gulf supply via Hormuz remains blocked. European OECD buyers (France, Italy, Netherlands) collectively took 3,904 kbd in 23–28 Mar, demonstrating continued backdoor dependence on Russian crude despite sanctions — with 56% of shipments moving on shadow-fleet tankers per CREA analysis.

Israel and South Korea both disappeared from Russia's crude buyer list after the early March weeks, consistent with sanctions compliance pressure and South Korea's pivot to US crude (5,647 kbd from the US in 16–22 Mar). The ESPO pipeline to China (~600 kbd continuous) provides Russia's most sanctions-proof channel; watch for further European buyer erosion if Hormuz transit normalises and Gulf barrels become available again.
russia_lng: Russian LNG exports exhibited a volatile 37.7% decline from 779 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 485 ktons (23–28 Mar), a counterintuitive contraction given that Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown has created the largest LNG supply deficit in history. The decline likely reflects logistics bottlenecks at Yamal LNG and the still-sanctioned Arctic LNG-2 project rather than demand weakness.

Japan's emergence as a top buyer (127 ktons in 23–28 Mar, up from 64 ktons in early March) validates ICIS LNG Edge reports of surging Asian spot demand for Russian cargoes. France and Netherlands — both significant buyers in early March (74 and 73 ktons respectively) — disappeared from the latest week, possibly reflecting EU political pressure to reduce Russian gas dependence even as Euronews reports European LNG cargoes are being lured to higher-priced Asian markets.

With Asian JKM spot prices up ~39% and European TTF up ~52% since the Qatar shutdown, Russian LNG producers face a structural arbitrage favouring Asian destinations. Spain (74 ktons) and Belgium (73 ktons) remain steady European buyers, likely locked into term contracts. The real question is whether Arctic LNG-2's constrained ramp-up can capture the massive margin opportunity before alternative supply sources or Hormuz reopening ease the deficit.
russia_lpg: Russian LPG exports are negligible — zero in both the first and latest weeks, with only Turkey receiving small volumes (19–21 ktons) in the middle weeks. This confirms that Russia is not a meaningful LPG exporter; its NGL production is primarily consumed domestically or processed into higher-value petrochemical feedstocks.

Despite Argus Media reporting that the Gulf conflict has marginally improved Russian NGL competitiveness in Asian markets, the infrastructure to scale seaborne LPG exports simply does not exist — Russia's contribution to filling the Middle Eastern LPG supply gap will remain immaterial.
russia_gasoil_diesel: Russian diesel exports dropped 70.1% from 5,610 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,675 kbd (23–28 Mar), a sharp contraction that contrasts with the crude export surge and suggests Moscow is retaining refined products domestically as Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian refinery infrastructure — the same attacks Reuters reports have halted 40% of export capacity — constrain refining throughput.

Turkey remains the dominant buyer but plummeted from 2,043 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 349 kbd (23–28 Mar), a 83% decline that may reflect Turkish refiners bringing domestic capacity back online after initial panic-buying, or shipping disruptions in the Black Sea. Brazil disappeared entirely after taking 1,157 kbd in early March — likely seasonal and freight-driven rather than geopolitical.

New destinations like Togo (260 kbd) and Egypt (262 kbd) emerged in 23–28 Mar, consistent with West African and North African buyers seeking Russian diesel as an alternative to disrupted Gulf-origin flows. The IEA's assessment that EU/US diesel supplies are protected assumes continued Russian seaborne flows — but with refinery infrastructure under sustained Ukrainian attack, this assumption is increasingly fragile.
russia_naphtha: Russian naphtha exports collapsed 61.4% from 3,925 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 1,514 kbd (23–28 Mar), with only Turkey (321 kbd) remaining as a buyer in the latest week. China, the dominant destination in early March (1,336 kbd), completely disappeared — consistent with Wanhua Chemical's FM declaration on Middle East supplies and possibly reflecting Chinese buyers pivoting to term naphtha from non-conflict sources.

India (1,084 kbd in 9–15 Mar) also dropped out, reflecting MRPL's FM declaration and 500 kbd refinery run cuts that have reduced naphtha import demand. South Korea and Indonesia — both with active petrochemical FM declarations (YNCC and Chandra Asri) — vanished after early/mid-March as crackers shut down entirely rather than source expensive spot naphtha.

The concentration onto Turkey as the sole remaining buyer is a vulnerability: if Turkish petrochemical demand softens or freight disruptions intensify in the Black Sea/Mediterranean, Russian naphtha exports could approach zero. This niche product flow is being squeezed from both supply (refinery attacks) and demand (Asian cracker shutdowns) sides simultaneously.
russia_sulphur: Russian sulphur exports are a low-volume, irregular trade — 31 ktons to Brazil (2–8 Mar), zero, then 71 ktons to Morocco (16–22 Mar), and 78 ktons to China (23–28 Mar). The 152% four-week increase is statistically meaningless given the tiny volumes and cargo-by-cargo variability.

China's appearance as the latest buyer (78 ktons) may reflect agricultural demand for sulphuric acid-based fertilisers, particularly as Iran agreed on 27 Mar to allow UN humanitarian and fertiliser shipments through Hormuz — suggesting a broader global fertiliser supply crunch that Russian sulphur exports could marginally help address.
us_crude: US crude exports have held remarkably steady at 25,500–27,500 kbd across all four weeks, reflecting near-maximum Gulf Coast terminal utilisation as the US delivers its 172 mb share (43%) of the IEA's historic 400 mb coordinated SPR release. EIA reported 3.322 mb/d for the week of 20 Mar, and much of the SPR release volume is being piped to Gulf Coast export docks for loading onto supertankers.

The buyer mix has rotated dramatically toward Asia: South Korea surged from 810 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 5,647 kbd (16–22 Mar) and 3,344 kbd (23–28 Mar) as Korean refiners (SK, S-Oil, GS Caltex, Hyundai) scramble to replace Gulf crude — South Korea's 22.5 mb SPR release is bridging but not solving its 93%+ Middle East dependence. India appeared as a new US crude buyer (1,207–1,741 kbd), consistent with MRPL and other Indian refiners diversifying away from disrupted Gulf supply chains.

European destinations (Netherlands, UK, France) remain significant but secondary, collectively taking ~6,000 kbd in the latest week versus ~8,000 kbd to Asia. Nigeria's disappearance as the top early-March destination (4,127 kbd) likely reflects a one-off trans-shipment or STS operation rather than structural demand. Spain's emergence (1,406 kbd in 23–28 Mar) aligns with Iberian pump price surges and refinery crude hunger.

The flat trend (+1.7%) masks churning beneath the surface: US export infrastructure is operating at full capacity, meaning further volume growth requires new terminal commissioning or extended SPR drawdown authorisation. With Brent at ~$92/bbl and an $18/bbl geopolitical risk premium embedded (Goldman Sachs), the WTI-Brent spread strongly incentivises continued maximum US exports.
us_lng: US LNG exports remained elevated but edged down 10.9% from 2,475 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 2,204 ktons (23–28 Mar), reflecting that Sabine Pass, Freeport, and Plaquemines are already running at maximum utilisation — there is limited spare liquefaction capacity to further ramp up despite the ~20% global supply gap from Qatar's 77 Mtpa shutdown.

The destination diversity is striking — 12 countries received US LNG in 23–28 Mar — with a clear Asia/Middle East tilt: Thailand surged to 240 ktons (from 67 ktons in early March), Egypt remained a consistent 148 ktons buyer, and Bangladesh appeared as a new destination (73 ktons), all consistent with Platts reports that the Qatar shutdown is pulling maximum utilisation from US facilities toward the highest-priced Asian markets.

The Netherlands emerged as the top destination (285 ktons in 23–28 Mar), likely reflecting Rotterdam's role as Europe's primary LNG regasification hub. Euronews reports that European LNG cargoes are increasingly being outbid by Asian buyers, meaning this European allocation may erode further. UK volumes disappeared entirely — a notable absence given UK gas market tightness.

India (73 ktons in 23–28 Mar) is actively negotiating alternative US cargoes after Petronet LNG's FM on Qatar supply, and South Korea returned as a buyer (73 ktons) despite KOGAS's new 10-year Trafigura deal for 3.3 Mtpa of US LNG. The structural story is clear: US LNG is the primary beneficiary of the Qatar crisis, but physical capacity constraints cap the upside.
us_lpg: US LPG exports held broadly flat (+4.2%) at ~1,000 ktons across the four weeks, demonstrating the resilience of Gulf Coast terminal operations at Enterprise and Targa despite the global supply shock. Japan remains the largest buyer but declined from 237 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 68 ktons (23–28 Mar), suggesting spot cargo competition from other Asian buyers is intensifying.

Indonesia surged to the top spot at 80 ktons (23–28 Mar), up from 72 ktons in early March, consistent with ICIS reports that Chinese PDH plant operators and Asian petrochemical buyers are scrambling for US and Australian cargoes at sharply higher prices as Middle Eastern NGL-linked LPG from Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Qatar remains offline.

South Korea dropped out of the top 5 in the latest week — notable given that YNCC has declared FM on ethylene production due to Middle Eastern naphtha suspension, which should theoretically increase LPG-as-alternative-feedstock demand. The Netherlands' appearance (61 ktons) suggests European buyers are also competing for US LPG barrels. The LPG-naphtha spread continues to favour increased US exports per Argus LPG World.
us_kero_jet: US jet fuel exports surged 48.2% from 1,074 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,592 kbd (23–28 Mar), the strongest growth rate of any US product flow in this batch — directly filling the gap left by halted Middle Eastern kerosene supply as European and global aviation faces acute shortages per OilPrice.com reporting.

The UK emerged as the top destination at 609 kbd (23–28 Mar), up from zero in early March, reflecting London's role as a major aviation hub drawing emergency jet fuel supply from the US Gulf Coast. Netherlands also appeared (302 kbd), likely for ARA blending and distribution across European airports.

The shift from a Caribbean/Latin American-focused trade (Panama 275 kbd, Trinidad 258 kbd in early March) toward a transatlantic trade underscores how profoundly the Hormuz crisis has reoriented global product flows. Mexico, traditionally the steadiest US jet fuel buyer (~270 kbd), disappeared in the latest week as transatlantic arbitrage economics dominate.
us_gasoil_diesel: US diesel exports grew 30.2% from 6,360 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 8,279 kbd (23–28 Mar), the largest absolute volume increase in the batch — reflecting the US Gulf Coast's role as the global swing diesel supplier as Middle Eastern distillate flows remain severed by the Hormuz closure.

The Netherlands exploded from 73 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 2,802 kbd (23–28 Mar), a 38x increase that positions Rotterdam as the primary European diesel redistribution hub. This aligns with the IEA's assessment that EU/US diesel supplies should be protected in the medium term, but this protection requires sustained transatlantic flows at historically elevated levels.

Australia appeared as a consistent new buyer at 300–304 kbd across the last three weeks, reflecting the Asia-Pacific diesel supply gap as Middle Eastern and Russian flows contract. Morocco (304 kbd) also emerged, consistent with North African buyers pivoting from Gulf-origin to US-origin diesel. The UK remained a major destination (903 kbd), and Trinidad and Tobago returned (300 kbd), likely for Caribbean redistribution.

With the IEA coordinated SPR release focusing primarily on crude, the US refinery complex is simultaneously processing record crude inputs and maximising product exports — a dual role that will test Gulf Coast logistics capacity if sustained beyond Q1.
us_gasoline: US gasoline exports rose 11.9% from 4,952 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 5,540 kbd (23–28 Mar), maintaining the Western Hemisphere focus that characterises this flow. Mexico remained the dominant destination throughout but declined from 1,829 kbd to 1,066 kbd — a 42% drop consistent with peso depreciation and demand destruction from elevated fuel prices in Mexico.

Australia appeared as a new top-5 destination at 303 kbd (23–28 Mar), an unusual transpacific gasoline flow driven by the disruption of Australia's traditional Singapore-sourced gasoline supply chain, which itself depends on Middle Eastern crude feedstocks. Colombia rebounded to 426 kbd after being absent in mid-March weeks.

The Ecuador and Guatemala disappearance (606 kbd and 487 kbd respectively in early March) reflects Central/South American buyer destocking after initial panic purchases in the first week of the crisis. The Latin American focus of US gasoline exports — unlike diesel, which has pivoted transatlantic — reflects the product's lower value density making long-haul shipments to Europe less economic.
us_naphtha: US naphtha exports rose 12.6% from 2,337 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 2,631 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the flow increasingly concentrated on Latin American petrochemical demand. Venezuela remained the single largest buyer across all weeks (464–1,538 kbd), reflecting the ongoing US licence for diluent/naphtha supply to Venezuelan heavy crude operations despite broader sanctions.

Japan and South Korea appeared as significant Asian buyers in mid-March (797 kbd and 1,421 kbd respectively in their peak weeks) as Middle Eastern naphtha supply collapsed — YNCC's FM on ethylene production and Japan's 93.5% Middle East crude dependence created urgent spot naphtha demand. However, both declined in the latest week as US naphtha cargoes face a 25-30 day voyage to Asia versus 5-7 days for traditional Gulf suppliers.

Colombia's steady rise to 320 kbd (23–28 Mar) suggests Ecopetrol and Colombian petrochemical complexes are successfully locking in US-sourced feedstock. Watch for Asian buyers to increase US naphtha purchases if the partial Hormuz reopening fails to restore Middle Eastern naphtha supply — the freight disadvantage may be offset by the sheer absence of alternatives.
us_sulphur: US sulphur exports are negligible and sporadic — 14 ktons to Mexico (9–15 Mar) and 44 ktons to Belgium (16–22 Mar), with zero volumes in the first and last weeks. This is not a trade flow driven by the Gulf crisis; US sulphur production is a refinery byproduct that moves in irregular spot cargoes.
australia_crude: Australian crude exports declined 31.6% from 1,867 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,277 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the destination mix narrowing dramatically to a single buyer: South Korea (743 kbd). This concentration reflects South Korea's desperate scramble for non-Gulf crude as its refiners face 93%+ Middle East dependence — South Korea's 22.5 mb SPR release (5 mb drawn by 25 Mar per Yonhap) provides only temporary relief.

Indonesia (650 kbd) and Singapore (647 kbd), the top early-March buyers, both disappeared from the latest week as these Southeast Asian refiners likely pivoted to cheaper Russian or West African alternatives. Thailand also dropped out after receiving 570 kbd in early March. Australia's small crude export base (~200-300 kbd typical) constrains its ability to meaningfully offset Gulf supply losses despite its non-Hormuz geographic advantage.

The Brunei cargo (650 kbd in 9–15 Mar) was likely a one-off condensate transfer related to LNG processing rather than a structural trade. Australia's crude export role in this crisis is supplementary rather than transformative — its real strategic value lies in LNG.
australia_lng: Australian LNG exports peaked at 1,760 ktons (9–15 Mar) before declining to 940 ktons (23–28 Mar), a 32.7% contraction that reflects not demand weakness but approaching physical production limits. With Qatar's 77 Mtpa offline — removing ~20% of global LNG supply — Australia's installed capacity at NWS, Gorgon, Wheatstone, GLNG, APLNG, and Ichthys is running at maximum, but Kpler analysis confirms realistic supplementary supply totals under 2 Mtpa against a 5.8 Mtpa monthly shortfall.

Japan remained the anchor buyer at 422 ktons (23–28 Mar), down from 583 ktons in early March, consistent with deliberate demand rationing and nuclear restart progress providing some offset. South Korea declined from 200 to 104 ktons, while China dropped from 404 to 143 ktons — suggesting Chinese buyers are rotating toward Russian Yamal cargoes or pipeline gas via Power of Siberia, which provides partial insulation per Reuters.

India appeared as a new Australian LNG buyer (56 ktons in 23–28 Mar) for the first time in this dataset — a direct consequence of Petronet LNG's FM on Qatar supply and Gujarat Gas's 50% industrial supply cuts. Thailand also grew to 139 ktons, reflecting Thai Gas's declining domestic production and Myanmar pipeline constraints.

The structural story: Australia is the world's most important LNG supplier right now, but it cannot fill the Qatar gap. Asian spot JKM prices up ~39% will persist until either Qatar restarts or demand destruction forces equilibrium. Watch for Australian government intervention on contract diversion if domestic east-coast gas tightness intensifies.
australia_lpg: Australian LPG exports are minimal and irregular — 24 ktons to China (2–8 Mar), near-zero the following week, 45 ktons to China (16–22 Mar), then zero in the latest week. Australia is not a meaningful LPG exporter; its NGL production is largely consumed domestically or piped to petrochemical facilities.

China's intermittent purchases (24–45 ktons) may reflect spot cargo opportunism rather than structural demand, particularly as ICIS reports Chinese PDH operators are scrambling for US and Australian LPG at elevated prices. The 100% decline to zero in 23–28 Mar suggests available Australian LPG cargoes were exhausted or outbid by domestic buyers.
australia_naphtha: Australian naphtha exports are sporadic single-cargo flows — 345 kbd to Singapore (9–15 Mar) and 345 kbd to Malaysia (23–28 Mar), with zero volumes in the other two weeks. These are likely condensate-derived naphtha cargoes from NWS or other LNG-associated operations, not a structural export trade.

Malaysia's appearance as the latest buyer may reflect Petronas's petrochemical feedstock needs at their Pengerang complex, which traditionally sources naphtha from Middle Eastern suppliers now disrupted by the Hormuz closure. At ~345 kbd per sporadic cargo, this flow is too small to meaningfully impact the Asian naphtha supply deficit but does underscore how every available non-Gulf molecule is being redirected.
china_crude: China's crude imports have plunged 50% from 101,417 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 50,334 kbd (23–28 Mar), as the Hormuz closure has severed roughly half of China's seaborne Gulf supply — Gulf share collapsed from 44% to 29%. Iraq, which supplied 11,829 kbd in early March, has virtually disappeared (511 kbd) following the Iraq Oil Ministry's FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields, while Saudi Arabia has fallen out of the top-5 entirely.

Russia remains China's anchor supplier at ~14,142 kbd but has hit a ceiling — ESPO pipeline capacity (~600 kbd) and Kazakhstan-China (~220 kbd) are running at full utilisation, and Moscow lacks tanker capacity to meaningfully scale seaborne deliveries, according to Vortexa analysis. The Diplomat notes China's 1.3–1.4 billion barrels of strategic/commercial reserves provide ~4 months of cover, but drawdown has clearly begun.

Oman has emerged as the surprise gainer (+3,118 kbd), surging to 10,789 kbd in 23–28 Mar as its non-Hormuz export position via Mina Al-Fahal gives it a strategic advantage. Brazil (6,503 kbd) and Angola have also stepped in, but the geographic diversification is insufficient to offset the ~51,000 kbd shortfall. China's pre-crisis stockpiling (imports up 16% in Jan-Feb to ~12.47 mb/d) is now proving prescient.

Watch: If Hormuz remains closed into April, China faces a structural supply gap that reserves alone cannot bridge beyond Q2. The disappearance of Saudi and Iraq — together ~22,675 kbd in early March — represents the single largest supply dislocation China has faced since becoming a net crude importer.
china_lng: Qatar's complete disappearance from China's LNG supply marks a historic structural break — from 362 ktons (47.6% share) in 2–8 Mar to zero in 23–28 Mar. QatarEnergy formally extended its FM to Chinese contracts on 24 Mar, confirming the loss of the world's largest LNG supplier. China's Wanhua Chemical has separately declared FM on Middle East supplies, signalling cascading downstream impacts.

Total LNG imports fell 36% to 485 ktons (23–28 Mar), with Australia stepping up as the dominant replacement at 261 ktons (54% share). Malaysia, Indonesia, and Russia are filling the gap, but combined non-Qatar volumes cannot fully replace Qatar's ~360+ kton weekly contribution. Asian spot LNG prices have surged ~39% past $24–25/MMBtu, sharply increasing procurement costs.

Pipeline gas via Power of Siberia provides partial insulation — but LNG-dependent coastal industrial zones (Guangdong, Fujian, Zhejiang) face acute tightening. Canada, which supplied 28.5 ktons in early March, has also disappeared. The structural shift toward Australian and Southeast Asian LNG will persist well beyond any Hormuz reopening, as QatarEnergy's Ras Laffan restart timeline remains unknown.
china_lpg: China's LPG imports remain volatile but more resilient than other commodities, declining 16% from 544 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 457 ktons (23–28 Mar). The US has reasserted dominance at 235 ktons (52% share), up from a mid-March low of 85 ktons, as Gulf Coast terminals at Enterprise and Targa ramp shipments to fill the Middle Eastern void.

Qatar and Kuwait have both disappeared as suppliers, consistent with QatarEnergy's FM on all hydrocarbon exports and KPC's FM on crude (which curtails NGL byproduct flows). Iran continues supplying 107 ktons, likely via ship-to-ship transfers that bypass Hormuz, though this channel faces increasing disruption risk. PDH plant operators are scrambling for alternative feedstock at sharply higher prices, according to ICIS.

Australia has appeared as a new supplier (23 ktons in 23–28 Mar), signalling geographic diversification. But the world's largest LPG importer faces a structural tightening as Saudi, UAE, and Qatari NGL-linked supply — which collectively provided ~167 ktons (31% share) in early March — remains offline with no restart timeline.
china_gasoil_diesel: China's gasoil/diesel imports have collapsed 88% from 690 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 82 kbd (23–28 Mar), though this reflects intra-Asian trade dynamics rather than direct Gulf exposure — Gulf share was 0% throughout all four weeks. Japan and Chile have both disappeared as suppliers, leaving South Korea as the sole remaining source.

The decline likely reflects tightening product availability across Asia as regional refiners cut throughput in response to crude supply disruptions. South Korea's YNCC has declared FM on ethylene production due to total Middle East naphtha supply suspension, and broader Korean refinery run cuts may constrain exportable diesel surpluses going forward.
china_naphtha: China's naphtha imports illustrate the most dramatic supply chain restructuring in this crisis — Gulf share crashed from 76.8% (2–8 Mar) to 0% (23–28 Mar), a complete decoupling. Qatar, which supplied 1,201 kbd in early March, has disappeared entirely, along with Saudi Arabia, UAE, India, and Georgia.

Russia has surged as the top replacement at 742 kbd, joined by an unprecedented set of new suppliers: Norway (536 kbd), South Korea (345 kbd), and Thailand (332 kbd). This geographic scattering — from five Gulf-dominated sources to four non-Gulf origins — reflects desperate scrambling by Chinese petrochemical operators to secure feedstock at any price and from any origin.

Total imports still fell 31% to 1,956 kbd (23–28 Mar), as replacement volumes cannot match Gulf capacity. With YNCC (South Korea) and Chandra Asri (Indonesia) both under FM on petrochemical production due to naphtha supply loss, the entire Asian naphtha-to-petrochemicals chain is under severe stress. This is a supply chain that took decades to build around Gulf condensate — rewiring it will take months, not weeks.
china_sulphur: China's sulphur imports have declined 66% from 94 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 32 ktons (23–28 Mar), with only Canada remaining as a supplier. Saudi Arabia, which provided 55 ktons (58% share) in early March, has disappeared as Hormuz-linked flows ceased. The small volumes involved limit systemic impact, but China's fertiliser and chemical sectors rely on Gulf sulphur as a byproduct of oil/gas processing — a channel now structurally offline.

Russia briefly appeared as a supplier in 16–22 Mar (59 ktons) but dropped out the following week, suggesting spot cargo availability rather than systematic supply. Canadian volumes (32 ktons) are insufficient to meet China's industrial needs, pointing to inventory drawdowns and potential fertiliser production constraints if the disruption persists.
india_crude: India's crude imports have stabilised at ~30,440 kbd in 23–28 Mar after the sharp contraction to 25,069 kbd in 9–15 Mar when the Hormuz closure first disrupted Gulf supply chains. Russia has become the dominant supplier at 11,558 kbd (38% share) — up 40% from 8,263 kbd in early March — as Indian refiners aggressively pivot to ESPO and Urals barrels. MRPL, which declared FM on gasoline exports and shut its 100,000 bpd crude unit, has since received Russian crude to partially restore operations.

Iraq's collapse is the starkest signal of FM impact — from 4,574 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 427 kbd (23–28 Mar), consistent with the Iraq Oil Ministry's FM declaration and Basra output crashing to ~800 kb/d from 3.3 mb/d. Saudi Arabia has partially recovered to 6,660 kbd, likely via Yanbu pipeline-routed barrels bypassing Hormuz, while UAE re-entered at 3,400 kbd via ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah.

Geographic diversification has accelerated dramatically: Nigeria (2,888 kbd), Ecuador (2,111 kbd), and Colombia (1,900 kbd) have appeared as new suppliers, none of which featured in early March. The Gulf share partially recovered from a low of 5.8% (9–15 Mar) to 34.5%, but remains well below the pre-crisis 44%. India's government reports 25 days of crude inventory — adequate for now but eroding if Gulf flows don't resume.

Forward risk: India's refinery sector (5 mb/d capacity) is running below optimal utilisation due to crude mix constraints — Russian heavy sour grades are not a perfect substitute for Gulf medium-sour crudes that Indian refiners are configured to process. Margin compression is accelerating with Brent above $100/bbl.
india_lng: India's LNG imports have shown surprising resilience, rising 20% to 487 ktons (23–28 Mar) from 407 ktons in early March, despite the total loss of Qatar — which supplied 41.4% of India's LNG in 2025 (11.2 MT/yr). Petronet LNG has declared FM on its QatarEnergy contracts and downstream buyers, while Gujarat Gas imposed 50% supply cuts to industrial customers, triggering a 6.7% stock decline.

Oman has emerged as the critical replacement at 225 ktons (46% share), benefiting from its non-Hormuz LNG export position at Sur. The US appeared as a new supplier (135 ktons), along with Nigeria (60 ktons) and Angola (67 ktons) — an entirely new Atlantic Basin supply chain that did not exist for India two months ago. UAE and Mozambique, which supplied early March volumes, have both disappeared.

Spot LNG procurement costs have nearly doubled to $24–25/MMBtu, making replacement cargoes significantly more expensive than Qatar's long-term contract prices. India is actively negotiating alternative US and Australian term deals, but the near-term industrial gas shortage — particularly for Gujarat's petrochemical corridor — will persist until new supply agreements are finalised.
india_lpg: India's LPG imports have collapsed 62% from 210 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 80 ktons (23–28 Mar), threatening the Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala cooking fuel subsidy programme that serves 100+ million households. UAE, which was the largest supplier at 83 ktons (40% share), has completely disappeared alongside Bahrain and Saudi Arabia — all three shut by Hormuz closure and FM declarations.

The US has become India's lifeline at 52 ktons (65% share), with only a residual 28 ktons from Qatar. Gulf share cratered from 75% to 35%. The Indian government may need to source replacement volumes from US Gulf Coast terminals at significantly higher prices — the Economic Times reports procurement costs have surged as Middle Eastern NGL-linked supply remains structurally offline.

With Bapco (Bahrain) destroyed, KPC (Kuwait) under FM, and Saudi NGL throughput curtailed by offshore field closures, India's traditional LPG supply architecture has fundamentally broken. Rebuilding around US and potentially Australian supply will take 2–3 months of logistics restructuring at minimum.
india_gasoil_diesel: India's gasoil/diesel imports have shifted entirely from UAE to Russian supply — UAE provided 59 kbd (100% share) in 2–8 Mar but disappeared completely, replaced by Russia at 110 kbd in 23–28 Mar. The overall trend is rising (+86%), suggesting India is actively importing Russian diesel products to compensate for domestic refinery run cuts.

MRPL's FM on gasoline exports and partial shutdown of refining units has reduced India's domestic diesel output, creating import demand that Russia is filling with gasoil cargoes. This is a microcosm of the broader India-Russia energy relationship deepening under Gulf supply stress.
india_gasoline: India's gasoline imports remain small and solely sourced from South Korea, declining 40% from 75 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 45 kbd (23–28 Mar). The modest volumes reflect India's large domestic refining capacity, though MRPL's FM and refinery unit shutdowns have constrained domestic gasoline production.

South Korea's continued supply suggests Korean refiners are maintaining sufficient crude throughput to export products, though YNCC's FM on ethylene indicates petrochemical-grade naphtha shortages are beginning to impact the broader Korean refining complex. If Korean run cuts deepen, even this small Indian gasoline import channel could narrow.
india_naphtha: India's naphtha import supply chain has been completely rewired in four weeks. Gulf share crashed from 100% (2–8 Mar, with UAE at 511 kbd, Kuwait at 506 kbd, and Iran at 44 kbd) to 0% by 16–22 Mar, before partially recovering to 52% via Oman (324 kbd) in 23–28 Mar. Russia has emerged as a structural new supplier at 304 kbd.

Total volumes fell 41% to 628 kbd, with Kuwait's disappearance directly linked to KPC's FM and the Al-Ahmadi refinery drone strikes, and UAE's exit reflecting Hormuz closure. Oman's emergence as the primary Gulf supplier — from zero in early March to 324 kbd — underscores its strategic non-Hormuz advantage. Indian petrochemical crackers face feedstock tightening that will compress margins and potentially force production cuts.
india_sulphur: India's sulphur imports are intermittent and low-volume — Qatar supplied 33 ktons in 9–15 Mar (likely pre-FM cargo arrivals), then nothing until Canada (61 ktons) and Jordan (12 ktons) appeared in 23–28 Mar. The shift from Gulf to North American supply reflects the broader pattern of Indian importers scrambling for non-Hormuz sourcing across all commodity categories.
japan_crude: Japan is the most exposed major importer, with 93.5% Middle East crude dependence and no pipeline alternatives. Imports plunged 61% from 14,653 kbd (2–8 Mar, 100% Gulf) to 5,754 kbd (23–28 Mar, still 94% Gulf) — Japan has virtually no non-Gulf crude supply chain to fall back on. The 80 mb strategic reserve release (54 mb crude + 26 mb products) is bridging the gap, but drawdown at current rates leaves only ~4 months of cover.

Saudi Arabia, Japan's largest supplier, dropped from 8,929 kbd to 2,421 kbd (-73%), likely reflecting Saudi's own production curtailment with four major offshore fields shut since 9 Mar. UAE has held up better at 2,764 kbd, probably via ADCOP pipeline-routed barrels through Fujairah. Kuwait disappeared from the latest week, consistent with KPC's FM.

Malaysia (345 kbd) appeared as a new crude supplier — a tiny volume relative to Japan's needs but significant as Japan's first serious attempt at non-Gulf diversification. Qatar also appeared at 224 kbd despite its own FM, possibly representing pre-declaration cargoes arriving. Japan's structural vulnerability is existential: without Hormuz, Japan has no viable crude supply chain at scale.
japan_lng: Japan's LNG imports declined 36% to 840 ktons (23–28 Mar), but the supply base shows remarkable diversification compared to crude. Australia dominates at 516 ktons (61% share), with Malaysia (207 ktons), Papua New Guinea (70 ktons), and Russia (47 ktons) providing additional volumes. Qatar's share has effectively fallen to zero despite small residual cargoes in mid-March.

Japan's nuclear restart progress provides critical demand-side relief — each reactor restart reduces LNG burn by ~1 MT/yr — but winter 2026/27 exposure remains a serious concern if Qatar's 77 Mtpa stays offline. JERA and other utilities are locking in US spot cargoes at $24–25/MMBtu, roughly double pre-crisis contract prices.

Brunei dropped out as a supplier, and the overall portfolio has narrowed despite Japan's historically diversified LNG procurement strategy. The Gulf share was already low (0–16% across the four weeks), reflecting Japan's early pivot away from Qatar even before the formal FM extension. Key risk: Australian supply concentration — if any NWS or Gorgon maintenance events coincide with the Qatar outage, Japan faces a genuine supply emergency.
japan_lpg: Japan's LPG imports have risen 35% to 208 ktons (23–28 Mar), almost entirely from the United States (208 ktons, ~100% share). This near-total US dependence — Australia's 5 ktons in early March has disappeared — represents a completed supply chain pivot that preceded the current crisis, as Japan's Middle Eastern LPG contracts were already being wound down.

The US Gulf Coast monopoly on Japan's LPG supply is strategically stable but creates a single-origin concentration risk. Enterprise and Targa terminal throughput will be tested as Japanese, Chinese, Indian, and Korean buyers all compete for the same US LPG barrels at sharply higher prices.
japan_kero_jet: Japan's kerosene/jet fuel imports fell 26% from 658 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 490 kbd (23–28 Mar), with South Korea as the sole remaining supplier after China dropped out entirely (316 kbd in early March → 0). No Gulf exposure exists in this product — Japan sources jet fuel from regional Asian refiners.

China's disappearance as a supplier likely reflects Chinese refiners cutting product exports to preserve domestic supply amid their own crude import disruptions. South Korea's continued supply at 490 kbd indicates Korean refiners are prioritising product export contracts with Japan even as their own crude supply tightens.
japan_gasoil_diesel: Japan's gasoil/diesel imports are intermittent and low-volume — zero in both 2–8 Mar and 23–28 Mar, with a brief Brunei cargo (347 kbd) in 16–22 Mar. Japan's domestic refining capacity is generally sufficient for diesel needs, with occasional spot imports to balance seasonal demand. The crisis has not materially changed this pattern, though domestic refinery run cuts due to crude shortage could create import needs in April.
japan_gasoline: Japan's gasoline imports have surged 504% from 99 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 598 kbd (23–28 Mar), entirely sourced from South Korea — the sharpest increase in any product category across all four Asian importers. This likely reflects Japanese refiners cutting crude throughput as Gulf supply dries up, forcing gasoline imports to meet domestic demand.

South Korea's ability to maintain and dramatically increase gasoline exports to Japan suggests Korean refiners are drawing on strategic reserves and US crude imports to sustain product output. However, if Korea's own crude imports continue declining (-62% over the four weeks), this gasoline export channel will eventually tighten, leaving Japan doubly exposed.
japan_naphtha: Japan's naphtha imports have held relatively steady at 1,322 kbd (23–28 Mar) vs 1,510 kbd in early March (-12.5%), but the supply structure has fundamentally changed. Gulf share fell from 100% (UAE 811, Qatar 451, Kuwait 247) to 71% — UAE remains dominant at 935 kbd (likely ADCOP-routed via Fujairah), but Qatar dropped from 451 to 237 kbd and Kuwait has disappeared entirely, consistent with KPC's FM.

New suppliers have appeared from unexpected origins: South Korea (165 kbd), Peru (131 kbd), and Algeria (90 kbd) — none of which supplied Japan's naphtha in early March. This geographic diversification is unprecedented for Japan's petrochemical sector, which has been structured around Gulf condensate for decades.

The relative resilience of UAE naphtha supply via ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah is a critical lifeline for Japan's petrochemical crackers, but Fujairah port has been hit by drone attacks, creating ongoing operational risk. If UAE naphtha flows are disrupted, Japan faces a petrochemical feedstock crisis.
south_korea_crude: South Korea's crude imports have declined 62% from 23,961 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 9,206 kbd (23–28 Mar), one of the steepest drops among Asian importers. The 22.5 mb SPR release (5 mb delivered by 25 Mar) is providing essential bridge supply, but at current draw rates, Korea's reserves will be exhausted well before any Gulf normalisation.

UAE has surged to become the top supplier at 4,120 kbd (45% share), overtaking Saudi Arabia (1,528 kbd) — likely reflecting ADCOP pipeline volumes through Fujairah as Hormuz-dependent Saudi cargoes decline. Saudi Arabia collapsed from 6,508 kbd to 1,528 kbd (-77%), the sharpest drop among Korea's traditional suppliers. Kuwait disappeared entirely, consistent with KPC's FM and the Al-Ahmadi refinery destruction.

The US, which ramped to 5,645 kbd in 16–22 Mar, pulled back to just 1,050 kbd in 23–28 Mar — suggesting available US export cargoes are being competed for across all Asian buyers. Algeria appeared as a new supplier (1,078 kbd), marking Korea's first significant North African crude procurement. YNCC's FM on ethylene production signals that crude supply constraints are already cascading into Korea's massive petrochemical sector (SK, S-Oil, GS Caltex, Hyundai).
south_korea_lng: South Korea's LNG imports fell 33% to 637 ktons (23–28 Mar) but the country's diversification strategy is providing relative resilience. Qatar dropped from 75 ktons (8% share) to zero, but Korea's 80% long-term contract coverage — including the new KOGAS-Trafigura 10-year deal for 3.3 mtpa of mostly US LNG — limits spot exposure compared to India or Japan.

Australia remains the anchor at 335 ktons (53% share), with Indonesia surging to 119 ktons (up from 45 ktons in early March) as a key replacement. China has appeared as a re-exporter (58 ktons), likely redirecting excess pipeline gas volumes as LNG — an unusual but pragmatic response to the supply crunch.

The government's assessment that Korea faces 'no LNG shortages' appears credible given the diversified portfolio and high contract coverage, but winter 2026/27 remains the stress test. If Qatar's 77 Mtpa remains offline through Q4, even Korea's well-hedged position will face tightening as seasonal heating demand surges.
south_korea_lpg: South Korea's LPG imports dropped 55% to 47 ktons (23–28 Mar), sourced entirely from the United States after Canada (12 ktons) disappeared. The zero Gulf exposure throughout all four weeks reflects Korea's pre-crisis pivot to US LPG — a strategic advantage that has insulated this particular supply chain from Hormuz disruption.

However, the volume decline itself is concerning: US supply fell from 92 ktons to 47 ktons, suggesting either logistics constraints or competition from other Asian buyers for US Gulf Coast LPG cargoes. Korea's PDH crackers and LPG-naphtha alternative feedstock operations face tightening supply at sharply higher prices as the global LPG-naphtha spread shifts under Gulf supply constraints.
south_korea_gasoil_diesel: South Korea's gasoil/diesel imports remain low-volume and volatile — from zero (2–8 Mar) to a peak of 436 kbd (16–22 Mar, mostly from Japan at 345 kbd) then back down to 72 kbd (23–28 Mar, from Russia 53 kbd and China 20 kbd). Korea's large domestic refining complex normally produces surplus diesel for export, so import volumes signal periodic supply gaps as refiners cut crude throughput.

Russia's appearance as a diesel supplier to Korea is notable — a channel that barely existed pre-crisis. China's small contribution (20 kbd) likely reflects product rebalancing within Northeast Asia as crude supply constraints force all regional refiners to optimise output across their complexes.
south_korea_gasoline: South Korea's gasoline imports have collapsed from 535 kbd (2–8 Mar, all from Japan) to zero in 23–28 Mar — a complete cessation. Japan's disappearance as a supplier in 16–22 Mar (replaced briefly by Singapore at 167 kbd) and then no imports at all suggests Korean refiners have restored sufficient domestic gasoline production, or demand destruction is occurring as pump prices surge.

The volatile pattern (Japan → Singapore → zero) reflects the fragility of intra-Asian product trade flows as every refiner in the region faces crude supply stress simultaneously. Korean refinery utilisation rates will be the key indicator — if they fall below 70%, product imports will need to resume.
south_korea_naphtha: South Korea's naphtha imports fell 48% from 4,655 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 2,408 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the most dramatic geographic restructuring of any Asian importer. Gulf share crashed from 56% to 30%, with UAE (-730 kbd) and Bahrain both disappearing — Bapco's Sitra refinery destruction (405 kb/d offline after drone strikes) eliminated Bahrain entirely, while Hormuz closure cut UAE flows.

The replacement supplier list reads like a geography textbook: Greece (436 kbd), Peru (345 kbd), Egypt (330 kbd), Algeria (372 kbd), and Libya (186 kbd) have all appeared — none of which supplied Korea in early March. This extreme geographic diversification underscores the desperation of Korean petrochemical operators (YNCC has declared FM on ethylene) to secure any available naphtha globally.

Oman has emerged as the most reliable Gulf supplier at 489 kbd, benefiting from its non-Hormuz position. Qatar still supplied 237 kbd, likely from residual pre-FM cargoes or Ras Laffan condensate stored outside the Gulf. Korea's massive steam cracker complex (~4 mt/yr ethylene capacity) faces the most acute feedstock crisis in its history — the shift from Gulf condensate to Mediterranean and South American naphtha introduces quality mismatches that will reduce cracker yields and margins.
thailand_crude: Thailand's crude imports fell 23% from 6,840 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 5,261 kbd (23–28 Mar), but the supplier mix has undergone a dramatic structural shift: Gulf share surged from 54% to 93%, driven almost entirely by UAE barrels routed via the ADCOP pipeline to Fujairah — UAE now supplies 4,905 kbd, or 93% of Thailand’s total crude intake.

Indonesia, the US, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and the Saudi-Kuwaiti Neutral Zone have all disappeared from Thailand’s supplier list by 23–28 Mar. Kuwait’s exit is directly tied to KPC’s FM declaration after the Al-Ahmadi refinery was struck by Iranian drones on Mar 19–20. The loss of US cargoes (1,000 kbd in early March) suggests long-haul barrels are being redirected to higher-premium buyers.

Thailand’s concentration risk is now acute: with 93% dependence on a single supplier (UAE) whose Fujairah loading operations have themselves been targeted by drone strikes, any further disruption to ADCOP pipeline flows or Fujairah port would leave Thailand with almost no crude supply. The government has suspended petroleum exports and is drawing on 61 days of reserves, but alternative supply diversification remains critically inadequate.

Watch for: Whether Malaysia (356 kbd in 23–28 Mar, the only non-UAE supplier) can scale up as a crude source, and whether Thailand can secure Brazilian or West African cargoes to reduce its dangerous single-supplier dependency.
thailand_lng: Thailand’s LNG imports collapsed 42% from 367 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 213 ktons (23–28 Mar) after Qatar — which supplied 192 ktons (52% share) in early March — went fully offline under QatarEnergy’s blanket FM declaration. This loss removes roughly half of Thailand’s LNG supply at a time when declining domestic gas production from the Gulf of Thailand and Myanmar pipeline has made the country increasingly import-dependent.

Thailand has scrambled to diversify: Mozambique (78 ktons) and Canada (72 ktons) appeared as new suppliers in 16–22 Mar, while Oman emerged at 70 ktons in 23–28 Mar — one of the few Gulf LNG producers still operating outside the Hormuz chokepoint. Australia has been the anchor supplier throughout at 63–76 ktons/week, providing critical baseline volumes.

The Thai Ministry of Energy is monitoring four scheduled March LNG deliveries, but with spot LNG prices up ~39% and Qatar’s 77 Mtpa fully offline, replacement cargoes come at severe cost premiums. Thailand has reportedly increased coal-fired power generation and reduced LNG-fired plant activity to manage the constrained supply — a retrograde step for energy transition goals but necessary for grid stability.
thailand_lpg: Thailand’s LPG imports are negligible — zero in three of the four weeks tracked, with only a 7 kton cargo from Malaysia in 16–22 Mar. Thailand has limited LPG import infrastructure and meets most cooking fuel and petrochemical feedstock demand through domestic production and relatively low Middle East dependence, insulating it from the worst of the Gulf NGL supply crisis hitting larger Asian importers like China and India.
thailand_gasoil_diesel: Thailand’s gasoil/diesel imports spiked 740% from 38 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 319 kbd (16–22 Mar) before settling at 57 kbd in 23–28 Mar, reflecting emergency procurement as domestic refinery margins compress under surging Brent crude prices. The single-week spike was almost entirely from India (319 kbd), likely opportunistic cargoes from Indian refiners running surplus product despite MRPL’s FM-related run cuts.

China, the sole supplier in early March at 38 kbd, has been replaced by Malaysia (57 kbd) as the primary diesel source in the latest week. Supplier rotation across China, Malaysia, and India over four weeks suggests Thailand is accepting cargoes on a spot basis from whoever can deliver, rather than operating under stable term arrangements — a sign of a tight regional product market.
thailand_gasoline: Thailand’s gasoline imports dropped to zero in 23–28 Mar after averaging 38–63 kbd from Singapore in the prior three weeks. The complete halt likely reflects a combination of Singapore’s own refinery supply constraints (PCS Singapore declared FM on petrochemical feedstocks) and Thailand’s petroleum export ban, which may have freed domestic gasoline volumes for internal consumption. If Singapore supply does not resume, Thailand’s 61-day reserve buffer will face additional drawdown pressure.
thailand_naphtha: Thailand’s naphtha imports have collapsed 97% from 796 kbd (2–8 Mar) to just 22 kbd (23–28 Mar), a devastating blow to the country’s petrochemical sector. Bahrain was the dominant supplier at 503–257 kbd through mid-March, but Bapco’s Sitra refinery was struck by Iranian drones on Mar 4 and Mar 9, leading to an FM on all operations and a complete halt of naphtha exports.

With Bahrain and US supply both gone, only a small 22 kbd cargo from Singapore appeared in 23–28 Mar. Asian petrochemical plants rely on the Middle East for 70–80% of naphtha feedstock, and the IEA estimates 70,000 b/d of naphtha is being diverted globally to substitute for halted LPG supply — further tightening the feedstock market. C&F Japan naphtha prices have surged to $756/mt from $623/mt. Thailand’s PTT Global Chemical and IRPC petrochemical operations face imminent run cuts if alternative naphtha supply cannot be secured.
vietnam_crude: Vietnam is the most critically exposed importer in this batch: crude imports have fallen from 2,000 kbd to zero between 2–8 Mar and 23–28 Mar — a 100% collapse. Kuwait, which supplied 100% of Vietnam’s crude through mid-March (primarily for the 200,000-bpd Nghi Son Refinery), has been completely cut off following KPC’s FM declaration and the destruction of Al-Ahmadi refinery infrastructure.

A single emergency 618 kbd cargo from Nigeria appeared in 16–22 Mar, but no replacement volume arrived in the latest week. Vietnam has cut its MFN oil import tariff to 0% via Decree No. 72/2026 and arranged 4 million barrels from international partners, but structurally replacing 80% of crude supply from a single Hormuz-dependent source is proving nearly impossible on short notice.
