Prime Minister Chính has placed urgent calls to Qatar, Kuwait, Algeria, and Japan, and signed an energy cooperation deal with Russia on Mar 23. However, with Nghi Son Refinery likely running at minimal or zero throughput, Vietnam is now entirely dependent on refined product imports from South Korea, Singapore, and Malaysia for its fuel needs — a precarious position for a fast-growing economy.
vietnam_lpg: Vietnam’s LPG imports surged from 3 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 71 ktons (23–28 Mar), a 2,267% increase driven entirely by US Gulf Coast cargoes replacing Chinese supply. China, Vietnam’s sole LPG supplier in early March, disappeared by the latest week as Wanhua Chemical’s FM declaration and Middle East feedstock disruption constrained Chinese availability.

The pivot to US LPG at 71 ktons/week represents a structural shift in Vietnam’s cooking fuel and petrochemical feedstock sourcing, though it comes at sharply higher prices given the global LPG-naphtha spread tightening. A mid-March transitional week saw diversified arrivals from the US (46 ktons), China, Saudi Arabia, and Malaysia, but the latest week shows complete US dependence — trading one single-supplier risk for another.
vietnam_kero_jet: Vietnam’s jet fuel imports fell 60% from 232 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 93 kbd (23–28 Mar), with the supplier mix narrowing dramatically. Thailand (102 kbd) and China (40–142 kbd) both disappeared from the supplier list, leaving Singapore as the sole remaining source at 93 kbd.

The loss of Thai jet fuel supply is linked to Thailand’s own petroleum export ban, while Chinese volumes dropped as refineries cut throughput. With Nghi Son Refinery likely offline due to the Kuwait crude cutoff, Vietnam cannot produce jet fuel domestically and is entirely dependent on Singapore’s constrained refinery complex for aviation fuel.
vietnam_gasoil_diesel: Vietnam’s diesel imports rose 50% from 672 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,007 kbd (23–28 Mar), reflecting the country’s emergency shift to product imports as domestic refining collapses. South Korea has become the dominant supplier, surging from 433 kbd to 920 kbd (+113%), as Korean refiners redirect surplus diesel to Southeast Asian buyers.

The spike in diesel imports is a direct consequence of the Kuwait crude cutoff: with Nghi Son Refinery unable to process crude, Vietnam must import all refined products. South Korea’s large refining complex (SK Energy, S-Oil, GS Caltex, Hyundai) is well-positioned to serve this demand, though their own Middle East crude supply is under strain from the same Hormuz disruption. Singapore provides a secondary source at 83–87 kbd.
vietnam_gasoline: Vietnam’s gasoline imports declined 81% from 442 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 82 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Malaysia (324 kbd in early March) completely disappearing as a supplier. Singapore is now the sole gasoline source at 82 kbd, down from a combined 442 kbd of Malaysian and Singaporean supply at the start of March.

The sharp decline suggests Vietnam may be rationing gasoline consumption or drawing on reserves, consistent with reports of fuel shortages across Southeast Asia including ‘out of stock’ signs at retail stations. With domestic refining offline and import volumes contracting, Vietnam’s fuel security situation is deteriorating rapidly.
vietnam_sulphur: Vietnam’s sulphur imports collapsed from 17 ktons (2–8 Mar, entirely from Qatar) to zero in 23–28 Mar. Qatar’s blanket FM on all hydrocarbon exports halted sulphur shipments — a byproduct of LNG and refinery processing. A single 19 kton cargo from Jordan appeared in 16–22 Mar as a one-off substitution, but no replacement supply has materialized since. Sulphur is critical for fertilizer production, and prolonged shortages could ripple into Vietnam’s agricultural sector.
united_states_crude: US crude imports fell 39% from 19,240 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 11,794 kbd (23–28 Mar, 6-day week), though the decline is partly a function of the shorter reporting period. The Western Hemisphere supply chain remains robust: Venezuela (2,319 kbd), Mexico (1,553 kbd), and Saudi Arabia (2,995 kbd via Yanbu/Red Sea) are the top three suppliers, with Saudi barrels likely arriving via the East-West Pipeline bypass route.

Iraq’s share has collapsed from 2,574 kbd (13.4%) in early March to just 279 kbd (2.4%) in 23–28 Mar, consistent with Iraq’s FM declaration on all foreign-operated oilfields and the shutdown of Basra export terminals. The US SPR release of 172 million barrels — the largest in history as part of the IEA’s coordinated 412 million barrel release — began deliveries on Mar 20, providing a critical buffer as Gulf supply tightens.

The supplier base has diversified significantly toward Latin America: Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Argentina, Mexico, Guyana, and Brazil collectively provide ~60% of US crude imports, demonstrating the strategic value of hemispheric energy partnerships. Bahamas (918 kbd, likely STS transfers) has become a consistent supply channel.
united_states_lpg: The US is a major LPG exporter, not an importer — LPG imports registered zero in three of four weeks, with only a single 12 kton cargo from Canada in 16–22 Mar. US Gulf Coast LPG export terminals (Enterprise, Targa) are running at maximum utilization to serve surging Asian demand as Middle Eastern NGL supply is curtailed.
united_states_kero_jet: US jet fuel imports surged 469% from 91 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 518 kbd (23–28 Mar), with South Korea emerging as the dominant supplier at 403 kbd. This is a remarkable reversal of traditional flows: Asian refiners, facing compressed crude supply but needing to maintain throughput, are redirecting surplus jet fuel to the US market where demand remains strong.

Taiwan appeared as a new supplier at 115 kbd in the latest week, likely reflecting CPC Corporation re-exporting surplus refined product as Taiwan’s own crude supply shifts to US barrels. The Canada-to-US jet fuel pipeline dropped from 91 kbd to zero, suggesting Canadian supply is being redirected or that US refiners are substituting Asian-origin cargoes at competitive prices.
united_states_gasoil_diesel: US diesel imports fell 58% from 1,256 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 533 kbd (23–28 Mar), with Turkey (373–446 kbd in early weeks) completely disappearing as a supplier — likely due to Hormuz-related disruptions to Turkish refinery crude supply and shipping route uncertainty. Canada remains the primary source at 477 kbd but has also declined from 883 kbd.

Argentina appeared as a new diesel supplier at 56 kbd in 23–28 Mar, reflecting the broader pattern of Latin American supply filling gaps left by Eastern Hemisphere disruptions. The overall import decline may also reflect the US SPR release beginning to ease domestic crude supply constraints, allowing US refiners to maintain diesel output.
united_states_gasoline: US gasoline imports fell 46% from 2,343 kbd (2–8 Mar) to 1,254 kbd (23–28 Mar). European suppliers have been hit hard: Netherlands (615 kbd) and Belgium (278 kbd) both disappeared entirely, likely because European refiners are retaining product domestically as their own crude supply chains face Hormuz-related disruption and TTF gas prices have spiked 52%.

Canada remains the anchor supplier at 683–930 kbd across the four weeks, while Bahamas (likely Caribbean transshipment) fluctuates between 112–750 kbd. India (345 kbd) and South Korea (173 kbd) appeared in 16–22 Mar as one-off emergency cargoes from Asian refiners — a highly unusual flow pattern reflecting the global scramble to redirect product volumes.
united_states_naphtha: US naphtha imports have undergone a complete geographic realignment: European suppliers (France, Netherlands, Belgium, Peru) provided 100% of the 844 kbd in 2–8 Mar, but by 23–28 Mar, all four have been replaced by South Atlantic sources — Angola (345 kbd), Argentina (106 kbd), and Brazil (101 kbd). Total volume fell 35% to 552 kbd.

France’s exit (–352 kbd) coincides with TotalEnergies’ FM declaration on 5.2 Mtpa of Qatar LNG offtake — which reduces NGL byproduct availability for French naphtha processing. The pivot to Angola and Latin America signals a structural shift in Atlantic Basin naphtha trade flows as European refiners hoard product and Asian naphtha prices surge to $756/mt, pulling cargoes eastward. Dow’s CEO has warned that petrochemical supply disruptions from the Iran war could fuel inflation through the rest of 2026.
taiwan_crude: Taiwan has executed one of the most dramatic crude supply pivots in this crisis: Gulf share collapsed from 99.3% (2–8 Mar, almost entirely Saudi at 2,600 kbd) to 0% in 23–28 Mar, with US crude now providing 100% of Taiwan’s 2,061 kbd imports. This validates Taiwan’s pre-crisis diversification strategy — US crude grew from zero to nearly 30% of Taiwan’s supply over the past decade, and the US may now be Taiwan’s sole crude supplier.

Saudi Arabia (–2,600 kbd) and UAE (–311 kbd) have both completely disappeared, while Kuwait (2,000 kbd in 9–15 Mar) provided a brief interim supply before KPC’s FM declaration cut that source off. The Atlantic Council notes that Taiwan’s 95% energy import dependency and 70% pre-crisis Middle East crude share made it among the most structurally exposed importers globally.

At 2,061 kbd (6-day week), current import levels are ~30% below the 2,931 kbd peak, suggesting Taiwan’s refineries are running below capacity even with US supply. CPC Corporation’s new Guantang LNG terminal (3 Mtpa, commissioned May 2025) and ongoing Alaska LNG investment discussions (up to 20 Mtpa) position Taiwan for better long-term supply security, but the near-term volume gap remains a critical vulnerability.
taiwan_lng: Taiwan’s LNG supply chain has been successfully restructured post-Qatar: total imports actually rose 43% from 350 ktons (2–8 Mar) to 502 ktons (23–28 Mar), even as Qatar (62 ktons, 18% share) was eliminated. Australia has become the anchor supplier, surging from 63 ktons to 290 ktons (58% share) — a 364% increase that reflects the redirection of Australian LNG cargoes to Taiwan at premium spot prices.

New and expanded suppliers include Equatorial Guinea (73 ktons, first appearance), Brunei (69 ktons), and Oman (70 ktons), demonstrating effective diversification across non-Hormuz sources. Oman LNG’s 10.4 Mtpa facility, located outside the Hormuz chokepoint, is commanding significant Asian premiums as one of the few Gulf LNG operations still running.

Gulf share has dropped from 40% to 14% (Oman only), but Taiwan’s overall LNG resilience is stronger than most Asian importers thanks to CPC’s proactive diversification and the new Guantang terminal capacity. The key risk is cost: with Asian spot LNG prices up ~39%, Taiwan’s power generation costs are rising sharply, particularly as nuclear phase-out plans leave the grid heavily dependent on gas-fired generation.
taiwan_gasoil_diesel: Taiwan’s diesel imports emerged from zero in 2–8 Mar to 211 kbd in 23–28 Mar, sourced from South Korea (129 kbd) and China (82 kbd). The mid-March spike to 675 kbd (entirely South Korean) suggests an emergency procurement pulse as Taiwan’s domestic refineries adjusted to new crude supply sources. The normalization to 211 kbd indicates CPC refineries are adapting to US crude feedstock, though product import supplementation continues.
taiwan_gasoline: Taiwan’s gasoline imports are minimal and sporadic — zero in three of four weeks, with only a 176 kbd cargo from Japan in 16–22 Mar. Taiwan’s domestic refining capacity (CPC and Formosa Petrochemical) is generally sufficient for domestic gasoline demand, and the one-off Japanese cargo likely filled a temporary gap during the crude supply transition from Saudi to US barrels.
taiwan_naphtha: Taiwan’s naphtha imports have collapsed to zero in 23–28 Mar from 1,231 kbd in early March, a 100% decline that threatens the island’s large petrochemical sector (Formosa Plastics, CPC’s Linyuan complex). UAE and Iraq provided 100% of naphtha in 9–22 Mar (892–664 kbd), but both sources have been cut off as Hormuz disruption intensified and Iraq declared FM on all oilfield operations.

Evergreen Marine’s FM declaration on all Gulf bookings and suspension of services to Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Iraq directly constrains Taiwan’s ability to receive Gulf-origin naphtha even if volumes were available. With Asian naphtha prices at $756/mt and 70–80% of regional petrochemical feedstock normally sourced from the Middle East, Taiwan’s crackers face imminent run cuts unless Singapore, US, or other alternative naphtha can be secured urgently.

This is arguably Taiwan’s most critical energy vulnerability after crude: the petrochemical sector is a major contributor to GDP and exports, and a prolonged naphtha shortage would cascade into plastics, synthetic fibers, and electronics manufacturing supply chains across the island.
